properties.name: Adirondacks
properties.note: A billion-year-old dome of Grenville basement rock — geologically a southern outlier of the Canadian Shield, not the Appalachians proper — pushed up by a young, still-rising hotspot.

properties.name: Green Mountains
properties.note: Vermont's spine — Precambrian and early-Paleozoic rock metamorphosed and stacked during the Taconic and Acadian orogenies.

properties.name: Catskills
properties.note: Not true mountains but a deeply dissected plateau — flat-lying Devonian sandstone and shale of the Catskill Delta, carved into peaks by streams.

properties.name: White Mountains
properties.note: New Hampshire's high country — resistant granite and schist intruded during the Acadian orogeny, home to the Northeast's tallest summits.

properties.name: Berkshires
properties.note: Western Massachusetts' rolling highlands — marble valleys and schist hills, a southern continuation of the Green Mountains.

properties.name: Poconos
properties.note: A glaciated escarpment of the Appalachian Plateau in northeastern Pennsylvania, edged by the Ridge-and-Valley front.

properties.name: Mahoosucs
properties.note: A rugged sub-range straddling the New Hampshire–Maine line, famed as one of the toughest stretches of the Appalachian Trail.

properties.name: Taconics
properties.note: A thin sliver of deep-ocean rock thrust dozens of miles westward over the continental margin during the Taconic orogeny — the type example of an allochthon.

properties.name: Hudson Highlands
properties.note: Where the Hudson River cuts a fjord-like gorge through ancient Grenville gneiss — part of the Reading Prong, the same basement as the Adirondacks.
